original_hypothesis.title: Gas-Based DoS on totalUptimeScore via Mass Validator Registration
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: ValidatorRegistry.totalUptimeScore() iterates over ALL validators for a virtualId in an unbounded loop. Combined with the permissionless addValidator (ECON-H003), an attacker can register thousands of dummy validators, making totalUptimeScore and any dependent functions revert due to block gas limits.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The loop in totalUptimeScore calls validatorScore for each validator, which makes external calls to the DAO. Thousands of validators would easily exceed the block gas limit.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unbounded for loop over validatorCount(virtualId) calling validatorScore which involves external calls

preconditions: totalUptimeScore is used by dependent protocol logic (rewards, ELO)
Attacker has gas for registering ~1000+ validators

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: totalUptimeScore iterates all validators with external call per iteration; gas cost grows linearly
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Register 5000+ unique addresses as validators via addValidator
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Denial of service on governance scoring and any reward distribution using totalUptimeScore

economic_questions: Can this permanently brick an agent's governance?
What is the cost of registering enough validators to exceed gas limit?
validation_requirements: Estimate gas per validatorScore call (includes DAO external call)
Calculate validators needed to exceed 30M gas block limit

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: ~0.5-2 ETH for gas on Base to register thousands of validators
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Griefing attack - no direct profit but disrupts agent operations

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All participants of the targeted agent
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Agent functionality and dependent reward systems

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test with 1000+ validators to confirm DoS

validation_result.mechanism: Unbounded loop DoS via permissionless validator registration
validation_result.impact_summary: Permissionless addValidator allows attacker to register thousands of validators for any agent, causing totalUptimeScore() and dependent functions to exceed block gas limits and permanently revert.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis identifies two independently verifiable code defects that combine into a DoS vector:

1. **Permissionless addValidator**: AgentNftV2.addValidator (AgentNftV2.sol) has no access control modifier. Any address can register any other address as a validator for any virtualId.

2. **Unbounded loop in totalUptimeScore**: ValidatorRegistry.totalUptimeScore (ValidatorRegistry.sol:52-57) iterates over ALL validators for a given virtualId. Each iteration calls validatorScore(), which internally calls _getScoreOf() -> _validatorScoreOf() -> IAgentDAO(dao).scoreOf(account), making an external call per iteration.

The _validators array grows unboundedly via _addValidator (ValidatorRegistry.sol:26-29), which pushes each new unique validator to the array. There is no mechanism to remove validators from this array.

Each iteration of totalUptimeScore involves: (a) storage read from _validators array, (b) storage read from _baseValidatorScore mapping, (c) external CALL to DAO contract's scoreOf function. Conservative gas estimate per iteration: ~8,000-15,000 gas. At Base's 30M gas block limit, approximately 2,000-3,750 validators would cause the function to revert.

The attacker only needs to generate unique addresses (trivially cheap) and pay gas for addValidator calls (~30-50k gas each on Base).
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Both defects are clearly present in the source code. addValidator has no access control, and totalUptimeScore has an unbounded loop with external calls. The _validators array has no removal mechanism and no size cap.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No access control modifier. Any address can register any other address as a validator for any virtualId. The only check is whether the validator is already registered (dedup), but each unique address is accepted.

Unbounded loop iterates over ALL validators. validatorScore() makes an external call to the DAO for each validator via _getScoreOf -> _validatorScoreOf -> dao.scoreOf(). With thousands of validators, this exceeds the block gas limit.
Each unique validator is pushed to _validators[virtualId] array with no upper bound check and no removal mechanism. This array is the one iterated by totalUptimeScore.
External call to DAO contract for each validator in the loop. This adds significant gas cost per iteration (~2600 gas for cold CALL + execution cost in dao.scoreOf).

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls AgentNftV2.addValidator(virtualId, uniqueAddress) - no access control check
2. isValidator check passes (returns false for new address)
6. Any call to totalUptimeScore(virtualId) now iterates 3000+ times
7. Each iteration: validatorScore -> _baseValidatorScore read + _getScoreOf -> _validatorScoreOf -> external call to dao.scoreOf
8. Total gas exceeds 30M block gas limit -> function reverts
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: AgentNftV2.addValidator (no access control) -> ValidatorRegistry._addValidator (pushes to unbounded array) -> ValidatorRegistry.totalUptimeScore (unbounded loop with external calls per iteration via validatorScore -> _getScoreOf -> AgentNftV2._validatorScoreOf -> IAgentDAO.scoreOf)

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects this vulnerability. The addValidator function has no access control regardless of deployment parameters.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation:
Attack is straightforward and cheap:
1. Attacker identifies a target virtualId (any active agent)
2. Attacker generates ~3000-5000 unique EOA addresses (trivial)
3. Attacker sends ~3000-5000 transactions calling addValidator(virtualId, uniqueAddress) from any account
4. On Base (L2), each tx costs roughly 30-50k gas at very low L2 gas prices (~0.001 gwei), so total cost is minimal
5. After registration, totalUptimeScore(virtualId) permanently reverts for that agent
6. Any protocol logic depending on totalUptimeScore is bricked for that agent

The attack is irreversible since there is no removeValidator function in the ValidatorRegistry.
validation_result.impact_assessment:
MEDIUM-HIGH severity:
- totalUptimeScore becomes permanently uncallable for the targeted agent
- Any on-chain or off-chain system relying on totalUptimeScore for reward distribution, ELO scoring, or governance weighting is disrupted
- The attack is irreversible (no validator removal mechanism exists)
- Attack cost is very low on L2 (Base)
- Each agent can be independently targeted
- The permissionless addValidator is itself a separate access control defect that enables this DoS
recommended_mitigations: Add access control to addValidator: restrict to DAO, founder, VALIDATOR_ADMIN_ROLE, or staking contract only
Add a maximum validator count per virtualId (e.g., MAX_VALIDATORS = 500)
Implement a validator removal mechanism (removeValidator) with appropriate access control
Consider computing totalUptimeScore off-chain or using incremental tracking instead of iterating the full array
If the loop must remain, implement pagination or gas-bounded iteration patterns
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy AgentNftV2 with a mock DAO that implements scoreOf. 2. Mint an agent (virtualId=1). 3. Loop calling addValidator(1, address(i)) for i in 1..5000 from any account. 4. Call totalUptimeScore(1) with gas estimation to confirm it exceeds 30M gas. 5. Verify totalUptimeScore reverts in a block-gas-limited context.
uncertainties: Exact gas per iteration depends on DAO implementation of scoreOf (not in scope), but even minimal implementations (~2600 gas for CALL + basic logic) are sufficient for DoS at ~3000-5000 validators

Whether totalUptimeScore is actively used in reward distribution logic (not fully visible in scope), though the function exists as a public view function in IValidatorRegistry interface suggesting it is depended upon
additional_testing_needed: Benchmark exact gas cost of validatorScore() with the deployed DAO implementation to determine precise validator count threshold
Identify all on-chain and off-chain consumers of totalUptimeScore to assess full blast radius
Test whether Base's actual gas limit differs from 30M and adjust threshold accordingly
